metadata.summary: Laravel patterns: thin controllers, Form Requests with authorization, API Resources, DTOs, DB::transaction, idempotent queue jobs, eager loading with with() for N+1 prevention.

content.title: Laravel Patterns
content.summary: Thin controllers that delegate to Action or Service classes; Form Requests for validation and authorization via Policies; API Resources for response shaping; DTOs for type-safe data flow through the service layer. Wrap multi-step writes in DB::transaction(). Make queue jobs idempotent with ShouldBeUnique. Never use $request->all() or $model->fill($request->all()); always call $request->validated(). Always eager-load relationships via with() to prevent N+1 queries. Use Attribute::make() (Laravel 9+) for accessors/mutators.

why.p: Laravel's Eloquent ORM produces N+1 queries invisibly when relationships are accessed inside loops without prior with() eager loading. $request->all() is a mass-assignment vector. Non-idempotent jobs double-charge or double-send when workers retry. These three failure modes are the most common agent-generated bugs in Laravel codebases and can be caught with Telescope SQL counts, PHPStan, and a DB::listen query budget in tests.

list.item: New Laravel 10/11 application with full-stack web or JSON API requirements.
Refactoring fat controllers into Service classes, Action classes (single-purpose __invoke), and Form Requests.
Wiring queue-backed jobs (Horizon + Redis), Eloquent scopes, accessors/mutators (Attribute API), and Form Request validation.
Standing up Pest/PHPUnit feature tests with RefreshDatabase and factories.
Adding API Resources for response shaping and Sanctum/Passport for authentication.
High-throughput async pipelines with strict tail latency — Laravel's per-request bootstrap is heavy; consider Octane only after profiling.
Lambda-style functions where cold start matters; Laravel Vapor mitigates but isn't free.
Microservice meshes where you need fine-grained per-service runtime — Symfony or Slim may be a better fit.
Pure data-pipeline batch jobs — a CLI in Symfony Console or pure PHP without the framework is leaner.

section.name: eloquent-model-patterns
section.p: Eloquent models are the centerpiece of Laravel ORM. They must declare fillable explicitly, use scopes for queries, and apply Attribute accessors/mutators for computed fields.

rule.statement: Always declare $fillable explicitly on models. Never use $guarded = [] (guards nothing). Never pass $request->all() to Model::create() or fill() — always use $request->validated() from a Form Request.
rule.rationale: Mass assignment via $request->all() sends all user-supplied fields to the model, including fields like is_admin or organization_id that should never be set by arbitrary request data.

rule.statement: Use Laravel 9+ Attribute::make(get:, set:) for accessors/mutators. Do not use the legacy getXxxAttribute / setXxxAttribute naming convention in new code.
rule.rationale: The Attribute API is explicit, testable, and IDE-friendly. Legacy magic methods rely on naming conventions that are error-prone and harder to discover.

example.title: Eloquent model with fillable, scopes, and Attribute API

rule.statement: Always eager-load relationships at the query layer (User::with(['organization', 'posts'])). Never access a relationship inside a foreach without prior with().
rule.rationale: Eloquent's lazy loading executes a separate query per relationship access. For N users, N+1 queries are issued. Using with() or packages like Bullet (with raise: true) catches this automatically in tests.

antipattern.reason: N+1: one SELECT per user to load organization. Use User::with('organization')->get() instead.

example.title: Reusable Searchable scope trait

section.name: service-layer-repository-pattern-and-dto-flow
section.p: Services wrap business logic and database operations. DTOs ensure type safety between layers. Repositories abstract data access and can be swapped for testing.

rule.statement: Wrap multi-step writes in DB::transaction(function () { ... }). Do not rely on save() call ordering. Never perform HTTP calls, queue dispatches, or mailer sends inside a transaction — they run before the commit and cannot be rolled back.
rule.rationale: Side effects inside transactions execute on a not-yet-committed state. If the transaction rolls back, the email is already sent or the HTTP call already made. Move side effects to after_commit callbacks or chained jobs.

example.title: Service with DB::transaction and side effects outside

rule.statement: Pass typed DTOs between controllers and services. Never pass $request->validated() arrays into the service — arrays lose type information and are unchecked by the IDE or static analyzer.
rule.rationale: An array is untyped. A DTO is a named, typed value object. PHPStan can check property access on DTOs; it cannot check string keys in associative arrays.

example.title: DTO pattern
Repository interface

section.name: http-layer-form-requests-api-resources-and-controller
section.p: Form Requests validate and authorize incoming requests. API Resources shape responses. Controllers remain thin, delegating to services or actions.

rule.statement: Form Requests must implement authorize() using a Policy. Never rely on controller-level checks alone. Always call $request->validated() — never $request->all() or $request->input().
rule.rationale: authorize() in Form Requests runs before the controller action. Checking authorization in the controller is too late; validation has already consumed the request body.

example.title: Form Request with authorization and toDTO()

rule.statement: Use $this->whenLoaded() for relationship fields in API Resources. Never call $resource->relationship directly in toArray() — it triggers a lazy load.
rule.rationale: whenLoaded() returns the relationship only if already eager-loaded. Direct access triggers a lazy query, defeating the purpose of eager loading in the controller.

example.title: API Resource with whenLoaded()
Thin controller delegating to service

section.name: pest-tests-idempotent-queue-jobs-and-gotchas
section.p: Pest provides expressive testing with RefreshDatabase for isolation. Queue jobs must be idempotent to handle retries safely. Common agent mistakes include N+1, mass assignment, and non-idempotent jobs.

example.title: Pest feature test with RefreshDatabase

rule.statement: Every queue job must implement ShouldBeUnique (Laravel 8+) or derive a unique key and check before performing the side effect. Jobs must be idempotent: running twice must produce the same result as running once.
rule.rationale: Queues retry jobs on failure. A non-idempotent job that double-charges a card or double-sends an email on retry causes real harm. ShouldBeUnique prevents duplicate dispatches.

rule.statement: Add $tries, backoff(), and failed() to every job class. Jobs without retry limits run indefinitely on permanent failures.
rule.rationale: Default Horizon and Laravel queue config may retry a failed job indefinitely. A job with a syntax error or a permanently invalid payload exhausts worker memory.

antipattern.reason: Mass assignment with all() sends every request field to the model, including non-fillable fields an attacker may inject. Always use $request->validated().

antipattern.description: Mail::to($user)->send(new WelcomeMail($user)) inside a DB::transaction block.
antipattern.reason: Mail sends before the transaction commits. If the transaction rolls back, the user record does not exist but the email is already delivered. Use deliver_later after the transaction.

antipattern.description: Resolving services via app(Service::class) instead of constructor injection.
antipattern.reason: app() bypasses the container's autowiring and breaks testability — the test cannot substitute a mock via the constructor.

template.purpose: DB::listen test helper that fails when query count exceeds budget (N+1 detector).
